skill.name: dast-ffuf
skill.description: Guides authorized ffuf-based dynamic application security testing for web content discovery, parameter fuzzing, virtual host checks, and CI security workflows.
skill.summary: Plan and run authorized ffuf web fuzzing workflows with clear filtering, reporting, and safety guidance.
skill.author: AgentSecOps

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, network, environment, script, and filesystem patterns, but review shows most are documentation examples or security-rule templates. The skill is still medium risk because it provides real ffuf network fuzzing and credential-fuzzing workflows that require authorization and rate limits. No prompt injection or confirmed malicious intent was found.

medium_findings.title: Dual-use Web Fuzzing Commands
medium_findings.description: The skill documents real ffuf workflows for directory discovery, parameter fuzzing, virtual host discovery, and CI testing. This is appropriate for authorized DAST work, but the same commands can be misused for reconnaissance against unauthorized targets.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command examples and the safety section confirm legitimate security-testing intent. Risk remains elevated because the network fuzzing behavior is directly usable outside authorized scope.

medium_findings.title: Authentication Endpoint Credential Fuzzing Guidance
medium_findings.description: The skill includes examples for fuzzing usernames and passwords against login endpoints. This is legitimate in a scoped test, but it is sensitive because it can resemble credential stuffing or brute-force activity if used without permission.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples explicitly target login requests and password wordlists. The surrounding DAST context reduces malicious-confidence, but the operational risk is clear.

medium_findings.title: Pipe-to-Shell Installer in CI Template
medium_findings.description: The CI template installs tfsec by piping a remote script directly into bash. This is a real supply-chain risk if copied as-is because remote script content is executed without pinning or verification.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line contains a direct remote download piped to bash. It appears to be a template example, not malicious code, but the execution pattern is dangerous.

low_findings.title: Static Hits Are Mostly Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: Many external command and URL findings are expected markdown examples for installing ffuf and running authorized web fuzzing. They are not hidden automation and do not execute when the skill is loaded.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The patterns are in fenced documentation blocks and match the stated purpose of an ffuf DAST skill. No evidence found that the skill silently executes them.

low_findings.title: Security Rule Template Contains Vulnerable Examples
low_findings.description: The rule template includes sample vulnerable code for hardcoded secrets, XSS, and weak cryptography so security rules can detect those patterns. These examples are educational content rather than active malicious behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding metadata labels the patterns as detection rules and remediation examples. No evidence found that these examples are executed by the skill.

low_findings.title: Environment Token Use Is GitHub Action Configuration
low_findings.description: The GitHub token reference is used by the Gitleaks action in a CI template. This is normal GitHub Actions configuration, not evidence of credential exfiltration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The token is referenced through GitHub Actions secrets and passed to a named secrets-scanning action. No external exfiltration endpoint is shown.

low_findings.title: XSS and API Key Examples Are Remediation Documentation
low_findings.description: The example document shows unsafe DOM operations and environment-based API key handling as part of security education. These snippets are not loaded by an application runtime in this skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The sections are labeled as vulnerability examples or secure API authentication examples. No evidence found of active script execution or secret theft.

dangerous_patterns.title: Remote Script Piped to Bash
dangerous_patterns.description: A CI template uses a remote installer with a pipe to bash. Pinning versions and verifying checksums would reduce supply-chain exposure.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is explicit and risky if copied into production CI. It is a template example, so it does not prove malicious intent.

dangerous_patterns.title: Network Fuzzing Against User-Supplied Targets
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill teaches ffuf requests against target hosts. This requires clear authorization because it can generate high request volume and reconnaissance traffic.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The behavior is central to the skill purpose and is clearly documented. Risk depends on user authorization and rate limits.

dangerous_patterns.title: Credential Fuzzing Workflow
dangerous_patterns.description: The authentication workflow includes username and password fuzzing examples. This pattern needs strict scope controls and low-rate execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command data targets login credentials. The DAST context makes it dual-use rather than confirmed malicious.

content.value_statement: Web teams need repeatable ways to discover hidden endpoints before attackers do. This skill turns ffuf workflows into structured, authorized DAST tasks with filtering and reporting guidance.
actual_capabilities: Explains ffuf setup and common command patterns for authorized web fuzzing.
Guides directory, file, API endpoint, and virtual host discovery workflows.
Shows parameter, header, cookie, and authentication endpoint fuzzing patterns.
Describes filtering, matching, rate limiting, and output format choices.
Maps testing activities to OWASP Web Security Testing Guide categories.
Includes CI and security-rule templates for reporting and governance.
limitations: Does not install or execute ffuf by itself.
Requires explicit authorization for every target before testing.
Cannot guarantee vulnerability confirmation without manual validation.
May create traffic volume that affects fragile systems if misconfigured.

use_cases.title: Pre-release Web Content Discovery
use_cases.description: Find exposed admin paths, backup files, and unexpected endpoints on staging systems before release.

use_cases.title: Bug Bounty Scope Testing
use_cases.description: Build rate-limited ffuf runs that respect program scope, filtering rules, and evidence requirements.

use_cases.title: CI Security Regression Checks
use_cases.description: Add lightweight ffuf checks for sensitive paths and publish artifacts for review.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Basic Directory Scan
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner workflow for a single authorized web application.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a cautious ffuf directory discovery plan for my authorized staging site. Include wordlist choice, status-code filters, rate limits, and evidence to collect.

prompt_templates.title: Tune Noisy Results
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate workflow for reducing false positives.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my ffuf result patterns and recommend filters for response size, words, lines, and status codes. Explain what each filter removes.

prompt_templates.title: Test Parameters Safely
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate workflow for GET and POST parameter discovery.
prompt_templates.prompt: Design an authorized parameter fuzzing workflow for this endpoint. Include safe payload handling, request headers, filtering, and manual validation steps.

prompt_templates.title: Build a CI DAST Gate
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced workflow for automated security testing.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a CI-friendly ffuf DAST strategy for staging. Include rate limits, output formats, sensitive-file checks, artifact retention, and failure criteria.

output_examples.input: Plan an authorized scan for hidden staging endpoints.
output: Recommended workflow: start with a small common-path wordlist, match successful and forbidden responses, and enable auto-calibration.
Safety guidance: limit concurrency, document target authorization, and review unusual responses manually before reporting.
Expected evidence: target URL, timestamp, wordlist name, filters used, discovered path, status code, and response size.

output_examples.input: Summarize noisy ffuf results for triage.
output: Likely noise: repeated responses with identical size and word count across random paths.
Priority findings: unique successful responses, forbidden admin paths, and files with backup or archive extensions.
Next step: verify each candidate path manually and avoid downloading sensitive data unless the engagement allows it.

output_examples.input: Create a CI policy for sensitive file exposure.
output: Policy result: fail the staging pipeline when sensitive paths return successful responses.
Reviewer action: inspect the generated report artifact and confirm whether exposure is intentional.
Governance note: retain scan artifacts for audit review and record approved exceptions.

best_practices: Confirm written authorization and test scope before running any fuzzing workflow.
Start with small wordlists, low concurrency, and clear filters before expanding coverage.
Validate findings manually and handle discovered credentials or PII through approved channels.
anti_patterns: Running ffuf against third-party systems without explicit permission.
Using large wordlists and high thread counts against fragile production services.
Treating every matched response as a confirmed vulnerability without validation.

faq.question: Does this skill run ffuf automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides workflows, command patterns, filters, and reporting guidance for users who run authorized tests.

faq.question: Is this suitable for production systems?
faq.answer: Only with approval, rate limits, monitoring, and a clear rollback plan. Staging is safer for routine testing.

faq.question: Can it confirm vulnerabilities?
faq.answer: It can help identify candidates. A human tester should validate impact, exploitability, and business context.

faq.question: What tools are assumed?
faq.answer: The primary tool is ffuf. The templates also reference common security tools such as Semgrep, Gitleaks, Trivy, and Checkov.

faq.question: How should sensitive discoveries be handled?
faq.answer: Minimize access, preserve evidence carefully, and report through the approved vulnerability or incident process.

faq.question: Can Claude, Codex, or Claude Code use this skill?
faq.answer: Yes. The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code as compatible assistant surfaces.
